key_questions: Prodrome: tingling, burning, itching at site before vesicles appear (herpes-specific)?
Location: lip/perioral (cold sore) vs intraoral (aphthous ulcer)?
red_flags: Eye involvement (herpetic keratoconjunctivitis) → urgent ophthalmology (can cause blindness)
Eczema herpeticum: HSV + eczema → widespread vesicular eruption — emergency
Immunocompromised patient → disseminated herpes — systemic antiviral urgently
Herpes encephalitis: headache + fever + confusion after cold sore → emergency
ddx: Aphthous ulcers (intraoral, not on vermilion border, no prodrome)

first_line.name: Topical Acyclovir/Penciclovir
first_line.drugs: Docosanol 10% cream (Abreva) q2–3h while awake × 5 days (OTC) | Acyclovir 5% cream (Zovirax) 5× daily × 4 days | Penciclovir 1% cream (Denavir) q2h × 4 days
first_line.notes: Best effect when applied at the PRODROME stage (tingling) before vesicles appear. Docosanol: OTC, blocks viral entry into cells. Acyclovir cream: modestly reduces duration by ~0.5–1 day. All topical agents: modest efficacy — reduce healing time by 1–2 days. Start at first symptom.

second_line.name: Oral Antiviral (moderate-severe or frequent recurrence)
second_line.drugs: Valacyclovir (Valtrex) 2g BID × 1 day (single-day regimen) | Acyclovir 400 mg TID × 5 days | Famciclovir 1500 mg single dose
second_line.notes: Oral antivirals significantly more effective than topical — reduce healing by 1–2 days and reduce pain. Valacyclovir 2g BID × 1 day: most convenient (single-day). Best if taken at prodrome. For frequent recurrences (≥6/year): suppressive therapy (valacyclovir 500 mg OD).

non_pharm: Apply at first sign of tingling/prodrome — most effective intervention is early treatment
Keep lesion clean and dry; avoid picking/squeezing
Avoid kissing and sharing utensils/lip products during active outbreak
Sun protection (lip balm with SPF) — UV exposure is a common trigger
L-lysine 1g OD (supplement) — weak evidence but safe; may reduce recurrence frequency
treatment.refer_when: Eye involvement; immunocompromised; eczema herpeticum; first episode with severe lesions; recurrent cold sores >6 per year (consider suppressive therapy); atypical presentation
